message.content:
Choosing between Python and Rust for a high-performance web backend is a classic trade-off between **development velocity** and **execution efficiency**. Both are excellent languages, but they serve different priorities.

Here’s a detailed comparison across key dimensions, followed by a decision framework.

---

## 1. Performance & Resource Efficiency

| Metric | Python | Rust |
|--------|--------|------|
| **Execution Speed** | Slow. Interpreted, dynamic typing, Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) limits multi-threading. | Extremely fast. Compiled to native machine code, zero-cost abstractions, no runtime overhead. |
| **Memory Usage** | High. Objects have significant overhead; garbage collection adds latency spikes. | Low. Manual memory management via ownership/borrowing; no garbage collector. |
| **Concurrency** | Limited by GIL. Use async/await (asyncio) or multiprocessing (heavy overhead). | Native async/await (tokio, async-std). Lightweight threads (OS threads) and async tasks scale to millions. |
| **CPU Bound Tasks** | Poor. Offload to C extensions (NumPy, Cython) or separate services. | Excellent. Ideal for CPU-intensive tasks (data processing, encryption, compression). |

> **Verdict**: Rust is **10–100x faster** in CPU-bound scenarios and uses significantly less memory. Python is suitable for I/O-bound tasks (DB calls, API requests) but struggles with heavy computation.

---

## 2. Development Speed & Productivity

| Metric | Python | Rust |
|--------|--------|------|
| **Learning Curve** | Low. Easy to read/write. Huge ecosystem of tutorials. | High. Steep learning curve due to ownership, borrowing, lifetimes, and type system. |
| **Time to MVP** |
